message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it. Read the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and find the code path / contract / test that makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot. Only conclude survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

Finding O-5: run_failed provenance entry omits experiment_run_id, so a failed run's trail cannot be correlated to its MLflow run
File: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:731
Description: On success, the terminal run_end entry records experiment_run_id (line 721), giving an auditor the cross-store link between the provenance trail and the MLflow run. On the error path the run_failed entry (lines 731-737) records error_type/message/last_phase but NOT experiment_run_id, even though run_id is in scope and the finally block does terminate the MLflow run as FAILED (line 754). A failed run is the case where correlation matters most (debugging an abort), yet the trail and the FAILED MLflow run share no recorded id. Combined with O-2 (the trail is not persisted/attached on the failure path either), a failed governed run leaves a FAILED MLflow run and an in-memory-only trail with nothing linking them.
PoC: Run run_study with a tracker and a plan whose discover() raises. The MLflow run is set to FAILED; the trail's run_failed entry has no experiment_run_id. Given only the FAILED MLflow run id, you cannot find its trail; given only the trail, you cannot find the MLflow run.

Return survives=false if you can refute it (with the specific reason), survives=true if it withstands a genuine disproof attempt.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

697	        if tuple(visited) != required:
698	            raise FirewallViolation(
699	                f"visited phase sequence {[p.name for p in visited]} != required "
700	                f"{[p.name for p in required]}"
701	            )
702	        if verify_trail:
703	            # Re-verify the whole chain before handing back the result. This re-hashes every
704	            # entry — O(N) — which is redundant for an in-memory trail this call built and never
705	            # mutated (each entry was hashed correctly when appended). It is a cheap safety net in
706	            # the common case but a real cost for very large trails, so a perf-sensitive caller
707	            # that trusts the in-process trail may pass verify_trail=False (pass 3, #F-036). The
708	            # important re-verification — of a trail deserialized from an external store — is the
709	            # caller's verify_records() on from_records(), which this flag does not affect.
710	            trail.verify()  # the provenance chain must be intact before we hand back the result
711	        # Terminal success entry (pass 3, #F-006): a phase 'report' record is not a run-level
712	        # terminus, so a trail truncated mid-run is otherwise indistinguishable from one that
713	        # completed. Record run_end ONLY after verify() passes, so its presence as the last
714	        # entry is an affirmative "this run finished with an intact chain" marker. The payload is
715	        # deterministic (no wall-clock) so the provenance-hash determinism guarantee holds — the
716	        # non-deterministic run duration goes to the experiment tracker as an SLO metric instead.
717	        trail.record(
718	            "run_end",
719	            f"study {defn.name!r} completed successfully",
720	            study=defn.name,
721	            experiment_run_id=run_id,
722	            n_provenance_entries=len(trail.entries) + 1,  # +1 counts this terminal entry itself
723	        )
724	        track(_count_logger("run_duration_s", time.monotonic() - started_at))  # SLO metric (#F-029)
725	        ok = True
726	    except Exception as exc:
727	        # Record a terminal failure entry BEFORE re-raising so the abort reason is recoverable
728	        # from the (caller-owned) trail, not lost (#109).
729	        last_phase = visited[-1].name if visited else "start"
730	        try:
731	            trail.record(
732	                "run_failed",
733	                f"{type(exc).__name__} during {last_phase}",
734	                error_type=type(exc).__name__,
735	                message=str(exc)[:2000],
736	                last_phase=last_phase,
737	            )
738	        except Exception as inner:  # noqa: BLE001 — never mask the original failure
739	            # The run_failed entry could not be recorded (payload unserializable, clock raised,
740	            # trail lock contended). Don't silently swallow — emit a structured warning so the
741	            # failure reason is at least in the logs, even though it is absent from the trail
742	            # (pass 3, #F-030). The original exception is still re-raised below.
743	            _log.warning(
744	                "could not record run_failed provenance entry (%s: %s)",
745	                type(inner).__name__,
746	                inner,
747	            )
748	        raise
749	    finally:
750	        trace_ctx.close()
751	        if tracker is not None and run_id is not None:
752	            try:
753	                # a failed run must be distinguishable from a successful one in the store (#110)
754	                tracker.end_run(run_id, status="FINISHED" if ok else "FAILED")
755	            except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001,S110 — best-effort cleanup
756	                pass
757	
758	    return StudyResult(
759	        study=defn.name,
760	        corpus_fingerprint=cfp,
761	        baseline=baseline,
762	        discovery_verdicts=discovery_verdicts,
763	        scorecard=scorecard,

765	        provenance=trail.entries,
766	        source_version=source_version,
767	        feature_matrices=tuple(feature_matrices),
768	        experiment_run_id=run_id,
769	    )

content.text: The finding is accurate about lines 731-737: the `run_failed` entry records `error_type`, `message`, `last_phase` but NOT `experiment_run_id`. Let me verify `run_id` is in scope and check whether `experiment_run_id` is recorded anywhere else on the trail (e.g., at run start), which would moot the correlation concern.

381	        bootstrap_tracing()
382	        corr_id = run_id or uuid.uuid4().hex
383	        trace_ctx.enter_context(run_trace_context(corr_id))
384	        # ---- INGEST ----
385	        with tracer.span("phase:INGEST"):
386	            enter(Phase.INGEST)
387	            try:
388	                corpus = defn.parser.parse(plan.source)
389	                source_fp = defn.parser.source_fingerprint(plan.source)
390	            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — normalize adapter/IO ingestion failures
391	                raise IngestionError(f"parser failed to ingest {plan.source!r}: {exc}") from exc
392	            # The parser Protocol return type is unenforced at runtime; a misimplemented adapter
393	            # that returns None/list/dict would otherwise slip past the normalizer above and die
394	            # at `corpus.units` with an opaque AttributeError, violating the documented
395	            # "Raises IngestionError on a bad source" contract (#134).
396	            if not isinstance(corpus, Corpus):
397	                raise IngestionError(f"parser returned {type(corpus).__name__}, expected Corpus")
398	            if not isinstance(source_fp, str):
399	                raise IngestionError(
400	                    f"parser.source_fingerprint returned {type(source_fp).__name__}, expected str"
401	                )
402	            if not corpus.units:
403	                raise IngestionError("ingestion produced an empty corpus")
404	            cfp = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
405	            source_version: str | None = None
406	            if versioner is not None:
407	                # The versioner is an optional seam; a storage failure (PermissionError,
408	                # OSError) must surface as the documented IngestionError, not a raw untyped
409	                # exception that the caller cannot distinguish from a fatal firewall breach
410	                # (pass 3, #F-008). Versioning is part of ingesting the source, so a failure to

content.text:
This test confirms exactly the finding's scenario: a failed run with a tracker (`start_run` returns `"r"`, `end_run` marks `"FAILED"`), and the `run_failed` entry's payload is asserted to contain only `error_type` — there is no `experiment_run_id` in it. The test even constructs `T` with `start_run` returning `"r"`, so a run_id exists and is in scope, yet the `run_failed` entry omits it.

I have thoroughly attempted to refute the finding and cannot:

1. The `run_failed` entry (lines 731-737) records only `error_type`, `message`, `last_phase` — confirmed by reading the code and the test at line 574-575.
2. `experiment_run_id` appears in the trail ONLY at line 721 (the success-path `run_end` entry). Confirmed by grep.
3. The `run_start` entry (line 344) is recorded BEFORE `run_id` is assigned (line 353), so it cannot and does not carry it.
4. `corr_id` (== run_id when tracker present) is used only for the trace baggage context (line 383) and is never recorded in the provenance trail.
5. The trail has no intrinsic id, and the engine never sets an MLflow tag/param carrying a trail identifier — so there's no reverse link either.
6. On the failure path the exception is re-raised (line 748), so the `StudyResult` (which carries `experiment_run_id`, line 768) is never returned.
7. The `finally` block does mark the MLflow run FAILED (line 754), so a FAILED MLflow run and an in-memory-only trail coexist with no shared recorded id — exactly as the finding states.

input.reasoning: Verified against the actual code; the finding holds. In src/assay_engine/pipeline.py the run_failed entry (lines 731-737) records only error_type, message, and last_phase — confirmed both by reading the code and by tests/test_pipeline.py:574-575, which asserts the payload's error_type with no experiment_run_id present. grep confirms experiment_run_id appears in the trail ONLY at line 721, the success-path run_end entry. The run_start entry (line 344) is recorded before run_id is assigned by tracker.start_run (line 353), so it cannot carry it. corr_id (which equals run_id when a tracker is present, line 382) is used solely for the trace baggage context (line 383) and is never written into the provenance trail. ProvenanceTrail.record() only stores explicitly-passed payload — no automatic trace/run_id capture — and the trail has no intrinsic id; the engine never sets an MLflow tag/param carrying a trail identifier, so there is no reverse link either. On the error path the exception is re-raised (line 748), so StudyResult (which does carry experiment_run_id at line 768) is never returned, while the finally block (line 754) still marks the MLflow run FAILED. Net result, exactly as the finding's PoC states: a failed governed run with a tracker leaves a FAILED MLflow run and an in-memory-only trail whose run_failed terminus shares no recorded id with that MLflow run. I could not find any code path, contract, or test that makes the correlation possible on the failure path.

content.text: Finding O-5 survives. The `run_failed` provenance entry omits `experiment_run_id`, and no other mechanism (run_start entry, trace corr_id, MLflow tag, or returned StudyResult) links the in-memory trail to the FAILED MLflow run on the error path.
